critique.summary: The article reports on the final investigation by ENTSO-E's Expert Panel into the April 28, 2025 blackout affecting continental Spain and Portugal. The panel identified multiple interacting technical factors causing the blackout and issued recommendations to improve system resilience and coordination across Europe.
mainPoints: The blackout on April 28, 2025, was caused by a combination of oscillations, voltage and reactive power control issues, differences in voltage regulation, rapid generator disconnections, and uneven system stabilization in Spain.
The blackout affected continental Spain and Portugal, with minor disruptions in a small area of Southwest France, but did not impact the wider European power system.
ENTSO-E established an Expert Panel of 49 members from TSOs, RCCs, ACER, and NRAs to investigate the incident following EU regulatory requirements.
The investigation followed a two-phase approach: a factual report released in October 2025 and a final report with root cause analysis and recommendations published in March 2026.
Recommendations focus on strengthening operational practices, improving monitoring and coordination, and adapting regulatory frameworks to evolving power system challenges.
The blackout was unprecedented in the Continental Europe Synchronous Area and highlights the importance of aligning market mechanisms and energy policies with physical system limits.
critique.truthfulness: The article appears accurate and factual based on publicly available information about ENTSO-E's role and procedures in investigating major power system incidents. The detailed description of the Expert Panel's composition, investigation methodology, timeline, and findings aligns with standard practices for such technical investigations. The blackout event itself is documented and the technical causes cited are plausible and consistent with known power system failure modes. The article cites official reports and regulatory frameworks, lending credibility to the information presented.
considerations: The article does not discuss the specific impacts on consumers or economic consequences of the blackout.
There is no mention of any independent or external expert review beyond the ENTSO-E Expert Panel.
The role of renewable energy sources or grid modernization efforts in the blackout is not addressed.
Potential political or regulatory responses following the recommendations are not covered.
The article does not provide perspectives from affected stakeholders such as national governments or industry groups.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around personal experiences during the Iberian blackout, the value of the detailed official report, and comparisons to other grid systems like Australia's. Commenters appreciate the comprehensive investigation and share anecdotes highlighting the blackout's impact and grid stability improvements elsewhere.

comments.author: singhrac
comments.text:
I think people underestimate how valuable these reports are, so I’m very glad that detailed investigation is done here. Every major grid operator around the world is going to study this and make improvements to make sure this doesn’t happen on their grid.

In a lot of ways it’s like investigations into airplane crashes.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly highlights the importance of detailed investigations for systemic improvements and the value of transparency in such reports.

comments.author: AnotherGoodName
comments.text:
Can’t read all of this since it’s 424 pages but i want to point out that Australia is beating Europe on grid connected storage. Not on a per capita basis. It’s beating all of Europe combined outright https://www.visualcapitalist.com/top-20-countries-by-battery...

We did have many many problems previously. The state of South Australia went out for a couple of weeks at one point in similar cascading failures. This doesn’t happen anymore. In fact the price of electricity is falling and the grid is more stable now https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2026/mar/19/power...

This price drop is inline with the lowered usage of gas turbine peaker plants (isn’t that helpful right now? No need for blockaded gas for electricity).

A lot of people say it can’t be done. That you can’t have free power during the day (power is free on certain plans during daylight due to solar power inputs dropping wholesale prices to negative) and that you can’t build enough storage (still not there but the dent in gas turbine usage is clear).

It’s one of these cases where you’ve been lied to. Australia elected a government that listened to reports battery+solar is great for grid reliability and nuclear was always going to be more expensive.
comments.analysis: The comment provides relevant context about grid storage and stability improvements in Australia, supported by references, adding useful comparative insight.

comments.author: darkwater
comments.text: The fact that there is not a single root cause but several ones makes me instinctively think this is a good report, because it's not what the "bosses" (and even less politicians) like to hear.
comments.analysis: This observation about multiple root causes aligns with best practices in incident analysis, suggesting a thorough and unbiased report.

comments.author: wedg_
comments.text:
I was supposed to fly home from Santiago de Compostella when the blackout happened. Me and my girlfriend had checked out of our hotel and headed to the bus stop to take the bus to the airport. The blackout had already started but we didn't realise (in hindsight, I do remember the pedestrian crossing not working. But I didn't think much of it). Anyways our flight was cancelled and it was clear we needed somewhere to stay the night.

I immediately rebooked the same hotel, but when we got back there the receptionist had left so you had to check in over the phone instead. Except WhatsApp wasn't working. Then mobile data went down. And before long we were walking through the old town going hostel to hostel looking for a place to sleep, as everything got darker and darker (due to the lack of powered street lighting). The old town in almost pitch black was pretty scary!

We ended up breaking back into the hotel, borrowing a bunch of towels from a laundry cart in the hallway and sleeping in this lockable room we found in the basement.

Besides that somewhat stressful part, it was a really strange but fun experience to see the city without power: no traffic lights, darkened shops with lots of phone lights, cafés still operating just with only outdoor seating and limited menus, the occasional loud generator, and most of all the people seemingly having a great time in spite of it.

I would've loved to have stayed out all night exploring the city, but finding somewhere to sleep that night was a bit more pressing!
comments.analysis: A vivid personal account that adds human perspective to the blackout's impact, though anecdotal and subjective.

comments.author: algoth1
comments.text: As someone who lived through the blackout it was wild. I felt back into the pre-internet, pre-smartphone era. It was pretty cool actually. The rumor mill spread so fast that Within hours the official word on the street was that we were getting hacked by a foreign military and people were joking that we had nothing of interest to be conquered xD
comments.analysis: This comment provides a relatable social reaction to the blackout, reflecting common rumor dynamics during crises.

comments.author: NooneAtAll3
comments.text:
If someone wants a "quick and dirty" answers - there's presentation linked https://eepublicdownloads.blob.core.windows.net/public-cdn-c...

page 11 contains "Full root cause tree" - one image with all the high level info
comments.analysis: Helpful pointer to a summarized resource, aiding readers seeking a concise overview.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text: 472 pages. That's going to be a nice bit of reading this weekend. It is very nice to see such a comprehensive report as well as the fact that it was made public immediately.
comments.analysis: Acknowledges the report's comprehensiveness and transparency, which are important for public trust.

comments.author: mythern
comments.text: That was quite the interesting read!
comments.analysis: A brief expression of interest without substantive content or analysis.

comments.author: wedge01
comments.text: 0.63 Hz and 0.2 Hz grid instability. Oh my.
comments.analysis: A cryptic comment referencing grid instability metrics without explanation, offering little value to the discussion.
